**ESPN** (an initialism of their original name, **Entertainment and Sports Programming Network** until 1985[[2]](#cite_note-ESPN_meaning-2)) is an American [basic cable](/wiki/Cable_television_in_the_United_States#Basic_cable "Cable television in the United States") [sports broadcasting](/wiki/Broadcasting_of_sports_events "Broadcasting of sports events") [network](/wiki/Television_broadcaster "Television broadcaster") and the flagship property of [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN_Inc. "ESPN Inc."), a joint venture of [the Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company") (72% and operational control via indirect subsidiary [ABC Inc.](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company")), [Hearst Communications](/wiki/Hearst_Communications "Hearst Communications") (18%), and the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (10%).[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) It was founded on September 7, 1979, by [Bill Rasmussen](/wiki/Bill_Rasmussen "Bill Rasmussen"), [Scott Rasmussen](/wiki/Scott_Rasmussen "Scott Rasmussen") and Ed Eagan.[[2]](#cite_note-ESPN_meaning-2)

ESPN broadcasts primarily from studio facilities located in [Bristol, Connecticut](/wiki/Bristol,_Connecticut "Bristol, Connecticut"), and also operates offices and auxiliary studios in [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"), [Orlando](/wiki/Orlando,_Florida "Orlando, Florida"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas"), [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"), [Charlotte](/wiki/Charlotte,_North_Carolina "Charlotte, North Carolina"), [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), and [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"). [James Pitaro](/wiki/James_Pitaro "James Pitaro") has been chairman since March 5, 2018, following the resignation of [John Skipper](/wiki/John_Skipper "John Skipper") on December 18, 2017.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

As of December 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit), ESPN is available to approximately 70 million pay television households in the United States — down from its 2011 peak of 100 million households.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Through [ESPN International](/wiki/ESPN_International "ESPN International"), it operates regional channels in Africa, Australia, Latin America and the Netherlands. In Canada, it owns a 20% interest in [The Sports Network](/wiki/The_Sports_Network "The Sports Network") (TSN) and its five sister networks. Despite the network's success, [criticism of ESPN](/wiki/Criticism_of_ESPN "Criticism of ESPN") includes accusations of biased coverage.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of ESPN](/wiki/History_of_ESPN "History of ESPN")

### Background and Launch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background and Launch")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/ESPN_HQ.jpg/250px-ESPN_HQ.jpg)](/wiki/File:ESPN_HQ.jpg)

Headquarters in [Bristol, Connecticut](/wiki/Bristol,_Connecticut "Bristol, Connecticut")

[Bill Rasmussen](/wiki/Bill_Rasmussen "Bill Rasmussen") came up with the concept of ESPN in May 1978, after he was fired from his job with the [World Hockey Association](/wiki/World_Hockey_Association "World Hockey Association")'s [New England Whalers](/wiki/Hartford_Whalers "Hartford Whalers"). Rasmussen and his ESPN co-founder Ed Eagan, joined by Rasmussen's son [Scott](/wiki/Scott_Rasmussen "Scott Rasmussen") (who had also been let go by the Whalers), first rented office space in [Plainville, Connecticut](/wiki/Plainville,_Connecticut "Plainville, Connecticut"). However, the plan to base ESPN there was put on hold because of a local ordinance prohibiting buildings from bearing rooftop [satellite dishes](/wiki/Satellite_dish "Satellite dish"). Available land to build their own facility was quickly found in [Bristol, Connecticut](/wiki/Bristol,_Connecticut "Bristol, Connecticut") (where the channel remains headquartered), with funding to buy the property provided by [Getty Oil](/wiki/Getty_Oil "Getty Oil"), which purchased 85% of the company from Bill Rasmussen on February 22, 1979, in an attempt to diversify the company's holdings. This helped the credibility of the fledgling company; however, there were still many doubters about the viability of their sports channel concept. Another event that helped build ESPN's credibility was securing an advertising agreement with [Anheuser-Busch](/wiki/Anheuser-Busch "Anheuser-Busch") in the spring of 1979; the company invested $1 million to be the "exclusive beer advertised on the network".[[7]](#cite_note-A-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/ESPN%27s_Old_Logo.png/250px-ESPN%27s_Old_Logo.png)](/wiki/File:ESPN%27s_Old_Logo.png)

ESPN's first logo, used from 1979 to 1985

ESPN launched on September 7, 1979, beginning with the first telecast of what would become the channel's flagship program, *[SportsCenter](/wiki/SportsCenter "SportsCenter")*. Taped in front of a small live audience inside the Bristol studios, it was broadcast to 1.4 million cable subscribers throughout the United States.[[9]](#cite_note-9) One month after launch, [Chris Berman](/wiki/Chris_Berman "Chris Berman") joined the network; he would continue to be an on-air fixture for decades.

### 1980s to 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1980s to 2000s")]

ESPN's next big step forward came when the channel acquired the rights to broadcast coverage of the early rounds of the [NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"). It first aired its games in March 1980, helping bring attention to what is today known as "[March Madness](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament")". The channel's tournament coverage also launched the broadcasting career of [Dick Vitale](/wiki/Dick_Vitale "Dick Vitale"), who, at the time he joined ESPN, had just been fired as head coach of the [Detroit Pistons](/wiki/Detroit_Pistons "Detroit Pistons").

In April of that year, ESPN began televising the [NFL draft](/wiki/NFL_draft "NFL draft"), bringing it to a mass audience and, over time, creating a television "event". That same month, the network began broadcasting [Top Rank Boxing on ESPN](/wiki/Boxing_on_ESPN "Boxing on ESPN"), marking the beginning of its involvement with televised [professional boxing](/wiki/Professional_boxing "Professional boxing").[[10]](#cite_note-10) The show lasted 16 years, and ESPN has since shown boxing live intermittently with other shows, including [ESPN Friday Night Fights](/wiki/ESPN_Friday_Night_Fights "ESPN Friday Night Fights") and others. For a period during the 1980s, the network had boxing tournaments, crowning champions in different boxing weight divisions as "ESPN champions".

The next major stepping stone for ESPN came over a couple of months in 1984. During this period, the [American Broadcasting Company](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") (ABC) purchased 100% of ESPN from the Rasmussens and Getty Oil.[[7]](#cite_note-A-7) Under Getty ownership, the channel was unable to compete for the television rights to major sports events contracts as its majority corporate parent would not provide the funding, leading ESPN to lose out for broadcast deals with the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (to [USA Network](/wiki/USA_Network "USA Network")) and [NCAA Division I college football](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_college_football "NCAA Division I college football") (to [TBS](/wiki/TBS_(American_TV_channel) "TBS (American TV channel)")). For years, the [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL"), [NBA](/wiki/NBA "NBA"), and [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") refused to consider cable as a means of broadcasting some of their games.[[11]](#cite_note-B-11) However, with the backing of ABC, ESPN's ability to compete for major sports contracts greatly increased, and gave it credibility within the sports broadcasting industry.

Later that year, the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court") ruled in *[NCAA v. Board of Regents of the University of Oklahoma](/wiki/NCAA_v._Board_of_Regents_of_the_University_of_Oklahoma "NCAA v. Board of Regents of the University of Oklahoma")* (1984) that the [NCAA](/wiki/NCAA "NCAA") could no longer monopolize the rights to negotiate the contracts for college football games, allowing each school to negotiate broadcast deals on its own. ESPN took full advantage and began to broadcast a large number of NCAA football games, creating an opportunity for fans to be able to view multiple games each weekend (instead of just one), the same deal that the NCAA had previously negotiated with TBS.[[11]](#cite_note-B-11) ESPN's breakthrough moment occurred in 1987 when it secured a contract with the NFL to broadcast eight games during [that year's regular season](/wiki/1987_NFL_season "1987 NFL season")—all of which aired on Sunday nights, marking the first broadcasts of Sunday NFL primetime games. ESPN's *[Sunday Night Football](/wiki/ESPN_Sunday_Night_Football "ESPN Sunday Night Football")* games would become the highest-rated NFL telecasts for the next 17 years (before losing the rights to [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") in 2006).[[12]](#cite_note-C-12) The channel's decision to broadcast NFL games on Sunday evenings resulted in a decline in viewership for the daytime games shown on the major broadcast networks, marking the first time that ESPN had been a legitimate competitor to NBC and CBS, which had long dominated the sports television market.

In 1992, ESPN launched [ESPN Radio](/wiki/ESPN_Radio "ESPN Radio"), a national [sports talk radio](/wiki/Sports_talk_radio "Sports talk radio") network providing analysis and commentary programs (including shows such as *[Mike and Mike in the Morning](/wiki/Mike_and_Mike_in_the_Morning "Mike and Mike in the Morning")* and *[The Herd](/wiki/The_Herd_with_Colin_Cowherd "The Herd with Colin Cowherd")*) as well as audio play-by-play of sporting events (including some simulcast with the ESPN television channel).[[7]](#cite_note-A-7)

On October 10, 1993, [ESPN2](/wiki/ESPN2 "ESPN2")—a secondary channel that originally was programmed with a separate lineup of niche sports popular with males 18–49 years old (with [snowboarding](/wiki/Snowboarding "Snowboarding") and the [World Series of Poker](/wiki/World_Series_of_Poker "World Series of Poker") as its headliners) as well as serving as an overflow channel for ESPN—launched on cable systems reaching to 10 million subscribers.[[7]](#cite_note-A-7) It became the fastest-growing cable channel in the U.S. during the 1990s, eventually expanding its national reach to 75 million subscribers.[[7]](#cite_note-A-7)

Ownership of ABC, and thus control of ESPN, was acquired by [Capital Cities Communications](/wiki/Capital_Cities_Communications "Capital Cities Communications") in 1985.[[13]](#cite_note-13) ESPN's parent company renamed themselves as Capital Cities/ABC Inc. Capital Cities/ABC Inc. was then acquired by [the Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company") in 1996[[14]](#cite_note-14) and was re-branded as [Walt Disney Television](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Television "Walt Disney Television").

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2000s")]

Challenges began to appear in the 2000s. ESPN began to shed viewers, more than 10 million over a period of several years in the 2010s even while paying large sums of money for the broadcast rights to properties like the NFL, [NBA](/wiki/NBA "NBA") and [College Football Playoff](/wiki/College_Football_Playoff "College Football Playoff").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

On April 26, 2017, approximately 100 ESPN employees were notified that their positions with the sports network had been terminated, among them athletes-turned-analysts [Trent Dilfer](/wiki/Trent_Dilfer "Trent Dilfer") and [Danny Kanell](/wiki/Danny_Kanell "Danny Kanell"), and noted journalists like [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL") [beat reporter](/wiki/Beat_reporting "Beat reporting") [Ed Werder](/wiki/Ed_Werder "Ed Werder") and [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") expert [Jayson Stark](/wiki/Jayson_Stark "Jayson Stark").[[16]](#cite_note-16) Further cost-cutting measures taken included moving the studio operations of [ESPNU](/wiki/ESPNU "ESPNU") to Bristol from [Charlotte, North Carolina](/wiki/Charlotte,_North_Carolina "Charlotte, North Carolina"),[[17]](#cite_note-17) reducing its longtime MLB studio show *[Baseball Tonight](/wiki/Baseball_Tonight_(MLB) "Baseball Tonight (MLB)")* to Sundays as a lead-in to the [primetime game](/wiki/Sunday_Night_Baseball "Sunday Night Baseball") and adding the [MLB Network](/wiki/MLB_Network "MLB Network")-produced *[Intentional Talk](/wiki/Intentional_Talk "Intentional Talk")* to [ESPN2](/wiki/ESPN2 "ESPN2")'s daily lineup.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

On April 12, 2018, ESPN began a supplemental [over-the-top](/wiki/Over-the-top_content "Over-the-top content") streaming service known as [ESPN+](/wiki/ESPN%2B "ESPN+").[[19]](#cite_note-verge-espnplusapril-19)

After having last carried national-televised [NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL") games in 2004, ESPN and ABC agreed in March 2021 on a seven-year contract to televise games, with some airing on [ESPN+](/wiki/ESPN%2B "ESPN+") and [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu"). The contract also awarded four of the seven Stanley Cup Finals to both ESPN and ABC. All other nationally televised games would air on [TBS](/wiki/TBS_(American_TV_channel) "TBS (American TV channel)") and [TNT](/wiki/TNT_(American_TV_network) "TNT (American TV network)") under a separate deal the league struck with [Turner Sports](/wiki/Turner_Sports "Turner Sports") the following month.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2020s")]

On August 8, 2023, ESPN and [Penn Entertainment](/wiki/Penn_Entertainment "Penn Entertainment") announced a deal to brand Penn's sportsbooks with ESPN branding. Penn's existing Barstool Sportsbook would be rebranded as [ESPN Bet](/wiki/ESPN_Bet "ESPN Bet") in late-2023.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

On February 6, 2024, ESPN announced a joint venture with [Fox Sports](/wiki/Fox_Sports_(United_States) "Fox Sports (United States)") and [TNT Sports](/wiki/TNT_Sports_(United_States) "TNT Sports (United States)") known as [Venu Sports](/wiki/Venu_Sports "Venu Sports"), including the three organizations' main linear sports channels and associated media rights. It was originally planned to launch in fall 2024.[[23]](#cite_note-23) However, following legal issues (including an antitrust lawsuit by [FuboTV](/wiki/FuboTV "FuboTV")), the service was ultimately cancelled.[[24]](#cite_note-THR2025-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) In May 2025, ESPN announced that it would officially launch an [ESPN-branded direct-to-consumer product](/wiki/ESPN_(streaming_service) "ESPN (streaming service)") later that year, which will incorporate ESPN's main channels and content from ESPN+, and become the main streaming offering for all ESPN subscribers.[[26]](#cite_note-:2-26)

On August 5, 2025, ESPN announced that it had reached an agreement to acquire the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League")'s NFL Media division, pending regulatory approval. Under the deal, ESPN would get control of the NFL's in-house media properties such as [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network"), [NFL RedZone](/wiki/NFL_RedZone "NFL RedZone"), and the league's official fantasy football service. The NFL would take a 10% equity stake in ESPN, NFL Network and RedZone would become part of the forthcoming ESPN streaming service, the NFL would license content from [NFL Films](/wiki/NFL_Films "NFL Films") to air on ESPN networks, and ESPN will reassign selected games from its NFL broadcast package to [NFL Network's exclusive game package](/wiki/NFL_Network_Exclusive_Game_Series "NFL Network Exclusive Game Series"). The NFL would continue to produce RedZone for ESPN, and the acquisition excludes properties such as NFL Films and [NFL+](/wiki/NFL%2B "NFL+").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) Regulatory approval was granted in January 2026, with the deal closing on the 31st of that month; NFL Media employees would join ESPN in April of that year.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

On August 6, 2025, ESPN announced that it had agreed to a five-year deal with [WWE](/wiki/WWE "WWE") to stream [WWE live premium events](/wiki/List_of_WWE_pay-per-view_and_livestreaming_supercards "List of WWE pay-per-view and livestreaming supercards") on the forthcoming ESPN streaming service, as well as simulcast select events on ESPN linear channels, starting with [Wrestlepalooza](/wiki/Wrestlepalooza_(2025) "Wrestlepalooza (2025)") in September 2025.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Programming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Programming")]

Main articles: [List of programs broadcast by ESPN](/wiki/List_of_programs_broadcast_by_ESPN "List of programs broadcast by ESPN"), [List of ESPN sports broadcasting rights](/wiki/List_of_ESPN_sports_broadcasting_rights "List of ESPN sports broadcasting rights"), and [List of UFC events](/wiki/List_of_UFC_events "List of UFC events")

Alongside its live sports broadcasts, ESPN also airs a variety of sports highlight, talk, and documentary-styled shows. These include:

* *[College GameDay](/wiki/College_GameDay_(basketball_TV_program) "College GameDay (basketball TV program)")* (basketball) – Weekly [college basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball") show airing from the *[Saturday Primetime](/wiki/Saturday_Primetime "Saturday Primetime")* game of the week site
* *[College GameDay](/wiki/College_GameDay_(football_TV_program) "College GameDay (football TV program)")* (football) – Weekly college football preview show airing from the site of a major college football game
* *[E:60](/wiki/E:60 "E:60")* – An [investigative](/wiki/Investigative_journalism "Investigative journalism") [newsmagazine](/wiki/Newsmagazine "Newsmagazine") program focusing on American and international sports
* *[First Take](/wiki/First_Take_(talk_show) "First Take (talk show)")* – A daily morning talk show with [Stephen A. Smith](/wiki/Stephen_A._Smith "Stephen A. Smith") and [Molly Qerim](/wiki/Molly_Qerim "Molly Qerim") (moved from ESPN2 on January 3, 2017)
* *[Get Up!](/wiki/Get_Up_(TV_program) "Get Up (TV program)")* – A daily morning show, focusing on the previous night's game results and the burning sports issues of the day
* *[Monday Night Countdown](/wiki/Monday_Night_Countdown "Monday Night Countdown")* – Weekly recap show aired on Monday evenings during the [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL") season, also serves as the pre-game show for *[Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football")*
* *[Pardon the Interruption](/wiki/Pardon_the_Interruption "Pardon the Interruption")* – A daily afternoon talk show where [Tony Kornheiser](/wiki/Tony_Kornheiser "Tony Kornheiser") and [Michael Wilbon](/wiki/Michael_Wilbon "Michael Wilbon") debate an array of sports topics
* *[SportsCenter](/wiki/SportsCenter "SportsCenter")* – The flagship program of ESPN, a daily sports news program delivering the latest sports news and highlights
* *[Sunday NFL Countdown](/wiki/Sunday_NFL_Countdown "Sunday NFL Countdown")* – Weekly preview show that airs on Sunday mornings during the NFL season
* *[The Pat McAfee Show](/wiki/The_Pat_McAfee_Show "The Pat McAfee Show")* – A daily afternoon talk show with news, opinion, and analysis

Many of ESPN's documentary programs (such as *[30 for 30](/wiki/30_for_30 "30 for 30")* and *[Nine for IX](/wiki/Nine_for_IX "Nine for IX")*) are produced by [ESPN Films](/wiki/ESPN_Films "ESPN Films"), a film division created in March 2008 as a restructuring of ESPN Original Entertainment, a programming division that was originally formed in 2001. 30 for 30 started airing in 2009 and continues airing to this day. Each episode is through the eyes of a well known filmmaker and has featured some of the biggest directors in Hollywood.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The *30 for 30* film *[O.J.: Made in America](/wiki/O.J.:_Made_in_America "O.J.: Made in America")* won the [Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Documentary_Feature "Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature") in [2017](/wiki/89th_Academy_Awards "89th Academy Awards"), the first such Oscar for ESPN.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[Ultimate Fighting Championship](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship") signed a five-year contract with ESPN starting 2019[[34]](#cite_note-34) on ESPN and ESPN+ which estimate every quarter 2 event on UFC on ESPN and 6 events on UFC Fight Night on ESPN+.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In March 2019, ESPN announced a new betting-themed daily program, *Daily Wager*, hosted by the network's gambling analyst Doug Kezirian.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The program was ESPN's first regularly scheduled program solely dedicated to gaming-related content. On May 14, 2019, ESPN announced a deal with casino operator [Caesars Entertainment](/wiki/Caesars_Entertainment_Corporation "Caesars Entertainment Corporation") to establish an ESPN-branded studio at [The LINQ Hotel & Casino](/wiki/The_LINQ_Hotel_%26_Casino "The LINQ Hotel & Casino") in Las Vegas to produce betting-themed content.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In order to help offset the impact of COVID-19 on its business, Walt Disney CEO [Bob Chapek](/wiki/Bob_Chapek "Bob Chapek") indicated during a fourth-quarter fiscal year 2021 earnings conference that the company would increase its presence in online sports betting, including in partnership with third parties.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

In 2023, *[The Pat McAfee Show](/wiki/The_Pat_McAfee_Show "The Pat McAfee Show")* moved to ESPN as part of a five-year, $85 million deal. The show replaced the Noon ET airing of *[SportsCenter](/wiki/SportsCenter "SportsCenter")* and *[This Just In with Max Kellerman](/wiki/This_Just_In_with_Max_Kellerman "This Just In with Max Kellerman").*[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

## Related channels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Related channels")]

### ESPN on ABC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: ESPN on ABC")]

Main article: [ESPN on ABC](/wiki/ESPN_on_ABC "ESPN on ABC")

Since September 2, 2006, ESPN has been integrated with the sports division of sister broadcast network [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"), with sports events televised on that network airing under the banner [ESPN on ABC](/wiki/ESPN_on_ABC "ESPN on ABC");[[42]](#cite_note-42) much of ABC's sports coverage since the rebranding has become increasingly limited to secondary coverage of sporting events whose broadcast rights are held by ESPN (such as [NBA](/wiki/NBA "NBA") games, [NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL") games, and the [X Games](/wiki/X_Games "X Games") and its related qualifying events) as well as a limited array of events not broadcast on ESPN (most notably, the [NBA Finals](/wiki/NBA_Finals "NBA Finals")).

### ESPN2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: ESPN2")]

Main article: [ESPN2](/wiki/ESPN2 "ESPN2")

**ESPN2** was launched on October 1, 1993. It carried a broad mix of event coverage from conventional sports—including [auto racing](/wiki/Auto_racing "Auto racing"), [college basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball") and [NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL") hockey—to [extreme sports](/wiki/Extreme_sports "Extreme sports")—such as [BMX](/wiki/BMX "BMX"), [skateboarding](/wiki/Skateboarding "Skateboarding") and [motocross](/wiki/Motocross "Motocross").[[43]](#cite_note-baltimoresun-espn2-43) The "[ESPN BottomLine](/wiki/ESPN_BottomLine "ESPN BottomLine")", a [ticker](/wiki/News_ticker "News ticker") displaying sports news and scores during all programming that is now used by all of ESPN's networks, originated on ESPN2 in 1995.[[44]](#cite_note-USA-44) In the late 1990s, ESPN2 was gradually reformatted to serve as a secondary outlet for ESPN's mainstream sports programming.[[45]](#cite_note-Deadspin-last_days-45)

### ESPNews

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: ESPNews")]

Main article: [ESPNews](/wiki/ESPNews "ESPNews")

**ESPNews** is a subscription television network that was launched on November 1, 1996, originally focusing solely on sports news, highlights, and [press conferences](/wiki/Press_conference "Press conference"). Since August 2010, the network has gradually incorporated encores of ESPN's various sports debate and entertainment shows and video [simulcasts](/wiki/Simulcast "Simulcast") of [ESPN Radio](/wiki/ESPN_Radio "ESPN Radio") shows, in addition to sports news programming. Since the 2013 cancellation of *Highlight Express*,[[46]](#cite_note-46) programming consists mainly of rebroadcasts of *SportsCenter*. ESPNews also serves as an overflow feed due to programming conflicts caused by sporting events on the other ESPN networks.

### ESPN Deportes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: ESPN Deportes")]

Main article: [ESPN Deportes](/wiki/ESPN_Deportes "ESPN Deportes")

**ESPN Deportes** (Spanish pronunciation: [[i.es.piˈen deˈpoɾtes]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"), "ESPN Sports") is a subscription television network that was originally launched in July 2001 to provide Spanish simulcasts of certain Major League Baseball telecasts from ESPN. It became a 24-hour sports channel in January 2004.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### ESPNU

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: ESPNU")]

Main article: [ESPNU](/wiki/ESPNU "ESPNU")

**ESPNU** is a subscription television network that launched on March 4, 2005, that focuses on [college athletics](/wiki/College_athletics "College athletics") including basketball, football, [baseball](/wiki/College_baseball "College baseball"), college swimming, and [ice hockey](/wiki/College_ice_hockey "College ice hockey").

### SEC Network

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: SEC Network")]

Main article: [SEC Network](/wiki/SEC_Network "SEC Network")

**SEC Network** is a subscription television network that launched on August 14, 2014, focusing on the coverage of sporting events sanctioned by the [Southeastern Conference](/wiki/Southeastern_Conference "Southeastern Conference"). Created as a result of a 20-year broadcast partnership between the two entities, the network is a [joint venture](/wiki/Joint_venture "Joint venture") between the conference and ESPN Inc., which operates the network.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-secdigitalnetwork1-50)

### ACC Network

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: ACC Network")]

Main article: [ACC Network](/wiki/ACC_Network "ACC Network")

Launching on August 22, 2019, the **ACC Network** is a subscription television network that focuses on the sporting events of the [Atlantic Coast Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference") as part of a current agreement extending to the 2036–37 academic term as a joint venture of network operator ESPN Inc. and the ACC.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### ESPN8 The Ocho

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: ESPN8 The Ocho")]

Main article: [ESPN8 The Ocho](/wiki/ESPN8_The_Ocho "ESPN8 The Ocho")

ESPN8 The Ocho is a special program block showcasing seldom-seen obscure sports that airs on the networks of ESPN Inc. The Ocho is also offered as a free ad-supported streaming television (FAST) channel on the Roku Channel, Prime Video and DirecTV Stream.

### ESPN+

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: ESPN+")]

Main article: [ESPN+](/wiki/ESPN%2B "ESPN+")

ESPN+ is an American over-the-top subscription video streaming service available in the United States, owned by the ESPN division of the Walt Disney Company, in partnership with ESPN Inc.

### Other services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Other services")]

ESPN HD

ESPN launched its [high definition](/wiki/High-definition_television "High-definition television") [simulcast](/wiki/Simulcast "Simulcast") feed, originally branded as **ESPNHD**, on March 30, 2003, with an [Opening Day](/wiki/Opening_Day "Opening Day") broadcast of the [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)") and [Anaheim Angels](/wiki/Anaheim_Angels "Anaheim Angels").[[52]](#cite_note-52) All current ESPN content is broadcast in high definition.

"Pardon the Interruption" and *Around the Horn* began airing in HD on September 27, 2010, with the relocation of the production of both shows into the facility housing the [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), bureau for [ABC News](/wiki/ABC_News_(United_States) "ABC News (United States)").[[53]](#cite_note-53)

ESPN broadcasts HD programming in the [720p](/wiki/720p "720p") resolution format, because ABC executives proposed a [progressive scan](/wiki/Progressive_scan "Progressive scan") signal that resolves fluid and high-speed motion in sports better, particularly during slow-motion replays.[[54]](#cite_note-54) The network's Digital Center itself natively holds [2160p](/wiki/4K_resolution "4K resolution") [UHD/4K](/wiki/Ultra-high-definition_television "Ultra-high-definition television") operations and equipment.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) In 2011, ESPNHD began to downplay its distinct promotional logo in preparation for the conversion of its standard definition feed from a [4:3](/wiki/4:3 "4:3") full-screen to a letterboxed format (via the application of the [AFD](/wiki/Active_Format_Description "Active Format Description") #10 display flag), which occurred on June 1 of that year.

[WatchESPN](/wiki/WatchESPN "WatchESPN")

WatchESPN was a website for [desktop computers](/wiki/Desktop_computer "Desktop computer"), as well as an [application](/wiki/Mobile_app "Mobile app") for [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") and [tablet computers](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer") that allowed subscribers of participating pay-TV providers to watch live streams of programming from ESPN and its sister networks (except for ESPN Classic), including most sporting events, on computers, mobile devices, [Apple TV](/wiki/Apple_TV_(device) "Apple TV (device)"), [Roku](/wiki/Roku "Roku") and [Xbox Live](/wiki/Xbox_Live "Xbox Live") via their [TV Everywhere](/wiki/TV_Everywhere "TV Everywhere") login provided by their cable provider. The service originally launched on October 25, 2010, as ESPN Networks, a streaming service that provided a live stream of ESPN exclusive to [Time Warner Cable](/wiki/Time_Warner_Cable "Time Warner Cable") subscribers.[[57]](#cite_note-Phillips1-57) [ESPN3](/wiki/ESPN3 "ESPN3"), an online streaming service providing live streams and replays of global sports events that launched in 2005 as a separate website,[[58]](#cite_note-58) was incorporated into the WatchESPN platform on August 31, 2011.[[59]](#cite_note-Nagle-59) Likewise, [ESPN+](/wiki/ESPN%2B "ESPN+") was launched in April 2018 as an add-on subscription for $4.99 per month.[[60]](#cite_note-60) On June 30, 2019, WatchESPN was discontinued with the service's full merger into the ESPN app.[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)

[ESPN Events](/wiki/ESPN_Events "ESPN Events")

ESPN Regional Television (formerly branded as ESPN Plus) is the network's [syndication](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication "Broadcast syndication") arm, which produces collegiate sporting events for free-to-air television stations throughout the United States (primarily those affiliated with networks such as [The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW") and [MyNetworkTV](/wiki/MyNetworkTV "MyNetworkTV") or [independent stations](/wiki/Independent_station_(North_America) "Independent station (North America)")). ESPN Plus syndicates college football and basketball games from the [American Athletic Conference](/wiki/American_Athletic_Conference "American Athletic Conference"), [Big 12 Conference](/wiki/Big_12_Conference "Big 12 Conference"),[[63]](#cite_note-63) [Mid-American Conference](/wiki/Mid-American_Conference "Mid-American Conference"), [Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference](/wiki/Metro_Atlantic_Athletic_Conference "Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference"), [Sun Belt Conference](/wiki/Sun_Belt_Conference "Sun Belt Conference") and the [Western Athletic Conference](/wiki/Western_Athletic_Conference "Western Athletic Conference").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

ESPN on [Snapchat](/wiki/Snapchat "Snapchat")

ESPN distributes various content on [Snapchat](/wiki/Snapchat "Snapchat") Discover, including a Snapchat-only version of *[SportsCenter](/wiki/SportsCenter "SportsCenter")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[ESPN MVP](/wiki/ESPN_MVP "ESPN MVP")

ESPN MVP (initially known as Mobile ESPN) was a 2005 attempt at operating a [mobile virtual network operator](/wiki/Mobile_virtual_network_operator "Mobile virtual network operator") with exclusive mobile content, first as a phone feature, then after its termination into a [Verizon Wireless](/wiki/Verizon_Wireless "Verizon Wireless") paid service. Technologies developed for it have since been transferred to the network's successful mobile strategy in the [smartphone](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") era.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Former services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Former services")]

ESPN Classic

Main article: [ESPN Classic](/wiki/ESPN_Classic "ESPN Classic")

**ESPN Classic** was a subscription television network that launched in 1995 as Classic Sports Network, founded by [Brian Bedol](/wiki/Brian_Bedol "Brian Bedol") and Steve Greenberg. ESPN Inc. purchased Classic Sports Network in 1997 for $175 million,[[64]](#cite_note-64) rebranding the channel as "ESPN Classic" in 1998. The channel broadcast notable archived sporting events (originally including events from earlier decades, but later focusing mainly on events from the 1990s and later), as well as sports documentaries and sports-themed movies. It was discontinued on December 31, 2021.

Longhorn Network

Main article: [Longhorn Network](/wiki/Longhorn_Network "Longhorn Network")

The **Longhorn Network** was a subscription television network that launched on August 26, 2011, focusing on events from the [Texas Longhorns](/wiki/Texas_Longhorns "Texas Longhorns") varsity sports teams of the [University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin").[[65]](#cite_note-65) It features events from the 20 sports sanctioned by the Texas athletic department, along with original programming (including historical, academic and cultural content). It was discontinued on June 30, 2024, a day before the Longhorns' move to the [Southeastern Conference](/wiki/Southeastern_Conference "Southeastern Conference").

## International channels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: International channels")]

Main article: [ESPN International](/wiki/ESPN_International "ESPN International")

ESPN owns and operates regional channels in Brazil, [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"), Netherlands, [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania") and [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"). In Canada, ESPN is a minority owner of [The Sports Network](/wiki/The_Sports_Network "The Sports Network") (TSN) and the French-language [Réseau des sports](/wiki/R%C3%A9seau_des_sports "Réseau des sports") (RDS). ESPN also has a minority stake in [J Sports](/wiki/J_Sports "J Sports") in Japan.

## ESPN Bet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: ESPN Bet")]

Main article: [ESPN Bet](/wiki/ESPN_Bet "ESPN Bet")

ESPN moved into the [sports betting](/wiki/Sports_betting "Sports betting") scene in November 2023 with plans to launch their sportsbook app "ESPN Bet" on November 14.[[66]](#cite_note-66) In a partnership with [Penn Entertainment](/wiki/Penn_Entertainment "Penn Entertainment"), ESPN Bet began in 17 states, and featured betting odds from their own sportsbook on their content.[[67]](#cite_note-67) A mutual early termination of the agreement, and a new contract awarded to [DraftKings](/wiki/DraftKings "DraftKings") commencing December 1, 2025, was announced on November 6, 2025.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ESPN&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Criticism")]

Main article: [Criticism of ESPN](/wiki/Criticism_of_ESPN "Criticism of ESPN")

ESPN has been criticized for focusing too much on men's college and professional sports (particularly the NBA and NFL), and very little on women's sports or extreme sports.[[69]](#cite_note-69) [Baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball"), [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"), and [soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") fans have also criticized ESPN for not giving their respective sports more coverage.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) Other criticism has focused on ethnicity in ESPN's varying mediated forms, as well as carriage fees and issues regarding the exportation of ESPN content.[[72]](#cite_note-afterword-72) Some critics argue that ESPN's success is their ability to provide other enterprise and investigative sports news while competing with other hard sports-news-producing outlets such as [Yahoo! Sports](/wiki/Yahoo!_Sports "Yahoo! Sports") and [Fox Sports](/wiki/Fox_Sports "Fox Sports").[[73]](#cite_note-73) Some scholars have challenged ESPN's journalistic integrity, calling for an expanded standard of professionalism to prevent biased coverage and conflicts of interest.[[74]](#cite_note-ethics-74)

On October 8, 2019, *[Deadspin](/wiki/Deadspin "Deadspin")* reported that an internal memo was sent to ESPN employees instructing them to avoid any political discussions regarding the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") and [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") in the aftermath of a tweet by [Houston Rockets](/wiki/Houston_Rockets "Houston Rockets") general manager [Daryl Morey](/wiki/Daryl_Morey "Daryl Morey").[[75]](#cite_note-75)
